sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “A Voice Calling in the Wilderness”

heb.paragraph: "Comfort, comfort (naḥamū) my people, says your God. Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and proclaim to her that her hard service has been completed, that her sin has been paid for, that she has received from the LORD’s hand double for all her sins" (vv.1–2). THE hinge of Isaiah. After 39 chapters of judgment, the music changes entirely. A voice cries: "In the wilderness prepare the way for the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God" (v.3). All four Gospels quote this for John the Baptist (Matt 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4–6; John 1:23). Every valley raised, every mountain lowered, the rough ground level, the glory of the LORD revealed (vv.4–5). "All people are like grass; their glory like wildflowers. The grass withers, the flower falls, but the word of our God endures forever" (vv.6–8). Peter quotes this in 1 Peter 1:24–25. "He tends his flock like a shepherd. He gathers the lambs in his arms and carries them close to his heart" (v.11).

notes.note: "Comfort, comfort my people." The doubled imperative: the comfort is urgent and emphatic. "Her sin has been paid for." The exile is atoning; its completion signals forgiveness.
"A voice of one calling: In the wilderness prepare the way for the LORD." All four Gospels apply this to John the Baptist. The wilderness highway: the same road-theology as 11:16; 19:23; 35:8.

"All people are like grass. The grass withers and the flowers fall, but the word of our God endures forever." Human transience versus divine permanence. 1 Peter 1:24–25 quotes this as the basis for the gospel’s reliability.

"He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arms." After the cosmic highway, the intimate image: God carrying lambs. The shepherd who built the highway uses it to carry the vulnerable.

refs.note: John the Baptist applies the wilderness-voice oracle to his own ministry, making Isaiah's highway imagery programmatic for the gospel.
Luke extends the quotation through verse 5, emphasizing that all flesh will see God's salvation — the universal scope of the new exodus.
Peter quotes the grass-and-flower imagery to contrast human transience with the enduring word that forms the gospel message.
The Baptist identifies himself as the voice crying in the wilderness, not the Word himself — a crucial Christological distinction.

hist.context: Isaiah 40 marks the theological hinge of the book, shifting from judgment oracles to consolation addressed to exiles in Babylon. The imperatives 'comfort, comfort' (naḥamū) are plural, summoning the heavenly council to announce that Jerusalem's warfare is ended and her iniquity pardoned. The highway-in-the-wilderness motif reimagines the exodus: just as YHWH led Israel through the desert from Egypt, so he will lead the exiles home from Babylon. The date is roughly 550–540 BC, as Cyrus's rise makes return imaginable.

sections.header: Verses 12–31 — “Those Who Hope in the LORD Will Soar on Wings Like Eagles”

heb.paragraph: "Those who hope (qāwâ) in the LORD will renew their strength. They will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and not grow weary, they will walk and not be faint" (v.31). The incomparability of God: "Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand?" (v.12). Nations are a drop in a bucket, dust on the scales (v.15). Lebanon’s forests are not enough fuel for his altar (v.16). "To whom will you compare me?" (v.25). "Lift up your eyes and look to the heavens: who created all these? He who brings out the starry host one by one and calls forth each of them by name" (v.26). "Do you not know? Have you not heard? The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He will not grow tired or weary" (v.28). Then the climax: eagles, running, walking. The order descends from soaring to walking — the greatest miracle is sustained ordinary faithfulness.

notes.note: "Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand? Who has held the dust of the earth in a basket?" Five rhetorical questions establishing God’s incomparability. No advisor, no teacher, no consultant.
"Surely the nations are like a drop in a bucket. Before him all the nations are as nothing." The political superpowers that terrorised Israel for 39 chapters: a drop. Dust. Nothing.
"Lift up your eyes. He brings out the starry host one by one and calls them each by name." God as astronomer: not just creating the stars but naming each one. Individual attention on a cosmic scale.

"They will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and not grow weary; they will walk and not be faint." The famous three-stage promise. Note the descent: soaring → running → walking. The hardest miracle is the last: walking without fainting. Ordinary endurance requires supernatural power.

refs.note: Paul's doxology echoes Isaiah's rhetorical questions about the incomparability of God's wisdom and counsel.
Paul quotes the 'mind of the LORD' language to argue that believers have received the Spirit who searches God's depths.
The psalm uses the same grass-flower metaphor for human frailty, forming an intertextual bridge between wisdom and prophetic traditions.
Paul's Areopagus speech draws on Isaiah's polemic against reducing the Creator to human-scale categories.

hist.context: The incomparability hymn (vv.12–26) employs rhetorical questions to demolish any notion that YHWH can be measured, advised, or compared. Nations are drops in a bucket; islands are dust on scales. The polemic has a specific target: Babylonian astral religion, which deified the stars (v.26). By naming and numbering the host of heaven, YHWH demonstrates sovereignty over what Babylon worships. The closing promise (vv.28–31) addresses weary exiles: waiting on YHWH renews strength when human energy fails.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVA voice says, “Cry out.” And I said, “What shall I cry?” “All people are like grass, and all their faithfulness is like the flowers of the field.ESVA voice says, “Cry!” And I said, “What shall I cry?” All flesh is grass, and all its beauty is like the flower of the field.NIVSpeak tenderly to Jerusalem, and proclaim to her that her hard service has been completed, that her sin has been paid for, that she has received from the Lord’s hand double for all her sins.ESVSpeak tenderly to Jerusalem, and cry to her that her warfare is ended, that her iniquity is pardoned, that she has received from the LORD’s hand double for all her sins.

rec.text: ""Comfort, comfort my people." The doubled imperative: the comfort is urgent and emphatic. "Her sin has been paid for." The exile is atoning; its completion signals forgiveness."

rows.label: Eagles — 40:12–31
rows.text: Nations = a drop. Stars called by name. "SOAR on wings like EAGLES; run and not grow weary; WALK and not faint."

hebtext.note: "Comfort, comfort (naḥamū) my people, says your God. Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and proclaim to her that her hard service has been completed, that her sin has been paid for, that she has received from the LORD’s hand double for all her sins" (vv.1–2). themes.note: Mercy AND Prophecy peak at 10: the hinge of Isaiah. "Comfort, comfort." All four Gospels quote v.3. 1 Peter quotes vv.6–8. Sovereignty at 9: nations are a drop. Faith at 9: eagles, running, walking. The most beloved chapter in Isaiah.

coaching.tip: 'A voice of one calling in the wilderness: Prepare the way for the LORD' (v. 3). All four Gospels apply this to John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4, John 1:23) — making it the most unanimously applied prophetic text in the New Testament. Isaiah wrote comfort for exiles; the Gospels heard a birth announcement. The wilderness road from Babylon becomes the wilderness road to Jesus.

'Those who hope in the LORD will renew their strength. They will soar on wings like eagles' (v. 31). Notice the sequence: soar, then run, then walk. The order is counterintuitive — it descends from dramatic to mundane. Isaiah's deepest wisdom is that sustained walking requires more strength than occasional soaring. Endurance, not ecstasy, is the hardest test of faith.
